posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
For many years, the first Friday of the year was set aside for our Over the Top lunch. it was a chance to pull out those bottles that were too precious or expensive to drink on a weekday, and the pain would be less if shared with friends.
We were younger then, with far greater capacity, or hollower legs, so we all brought two bottles, one the special one, the other something interesting which allowed the geek in us full rein. With 30 plus bottles, lunches tended to go on for five hours or so, either that or until they kicked us out. They petered out; a logistic and time sink hole.

Last Friday, an internet friend celebrated an early fiftieth birthday, so I put together an OTT tasting at a local restaurant, MP Taverna in Irvington NY. Although owner chef, Michael Psilakis grumbled about us making him cook French dishes, he did a stellar job, crowned by a brilliant Scallops in a Beurre Blanc sauce.

The wines were in good shape, but as many had serious bottle age, there were a few out of condition. We began with Champagne.

The Krug 2002 Clos de Mesnil was magnificent , my first Clos de Mesnil, and a very different animal to standard Krug, with its slightly oxidative signature. Here there was a powerful fruit component, a little grass and a finish for the ages.

The Ulysses Colin was a fine bottle, but stood little chance against the more precise yet joyous Krug.

The next flight was White Burgundy. I stopped buying it after too many premoxed bottles, but good as many of the reds were, the first two wines made me realize that I might perhaps have been a little hasty.

The 2001 Raveneau Clos was superb, very rich for a Chablis, showing mineral, apricot and grass. Made me wish I had bought some when it was affordable.

The owner of the Coche Enseigneurs 2009 had generously brought two bottles, with my nod going to the second one. Both though were showing beautifully, archetypal Coche, tangerine and floral, pairing perfectly with the scallops.

The 1962 Leroy Mersault Charmes was not in great shape, already old when first poured, and it then collapsed in the glass.

A 1972 Drouhin Corton Charlemagne showed very well at least initially, with notes of barley sugar and fruit but even this began to turn quite quickly.

Finally a friend opened up a bottle of Carillon Referts 2013. I had mentioned I had recently tasted and enjoyed the 2010, so he kindly opened a bottle. Essence of peach. While not as elegant as the first two wines, it did show well.

There was a lot of discussion as to whether to drink the Bordeaux before the Burgundies, and that is what we ended up doing. Served with a risotto of confit duck and oyster mushroom, again a fine match.

Lafleur 1998 was for me the clear winner, a really fine showing for a wine once described as too tannic for its own good. Whatever tannins there were, had integrated very well, and although the wines might have used a few more years, it was undoubtedly a great bottle approaching a three figure score.

Lafleur 1988 was also very fine, a little more measured than the 1998, and a lot less concentration… It showed some of the dry tannins of the vintage, but unlike many 1988s I have had, there was plenty of fruit acting as a counterweight.

Lafite 1989 was delicious, subdued initially but pairing it with the risotto gave it a chance to shine. A very layered wine, it combined with the earthy mushroom and duck flavors, ending up just behind the 1998 Lafleur.

A mystery flight served double blind, almost a homage to previous OTTs.

Chave 1983
Fairly controversial, although identified by one of the tasters as a Chave. Some complained about a slightly bitter greenness, not something I found, as I thought it a typical example of the wine. I think other bottles should be drunk soon.

Ridge York Creek Petits Syrah 1974.
I know this wine well, and it was a total brute for the first thirty years of its life. Now a glorious complex wine, still large boned and a little sauvage, it was among my favorite wines.

The 1975 Trotanoy proved the old adage it’s not the wine but the bottle. Unfortunately this particular bottle was DOA.


The first Burgundy flight served with simple lamb and a very concentrated jus.
I reversed the order beginning with the older wines, as they had been double decanted, and I was concerned that they had not held up.

Pierre Ponelle Bonnes Mares 1959 bottled by Bourrey. Ponelle I know, but was unfamiliar with Bourrey. A good wine, a little lost in the flight with the other two. A problem with the OTT format is that perfectly lovely wines that would grace any table can go missing, and this one did.

DRC Echezeaux 1986. A soft interesting fully mature Burgundy. Lovely and a good example of a DRC in a slightly minor key,

Not so the 1972 La Tache. Everything blazed with this wine, flavor, finesse, power and yet supremely elegant culminating in a long, easy finish. Our guest of honor did not disappoint.

The next flight of four featured Dujac and Mongeard Mugneret.

Dujac Bonnes Mares 2001 and Clos Saint Denis 1996.
If I had a metaphor for Dujac, it would be Sophia Loren. Upfront, generous and extremely beautiful with some pretty serious substance underneath. Both wines showed their Loren qualities, the 2001 bigger, rounder, showing bright red fruit, a hefty mid palate and a long easy finish. I have always loved the 1996, with its haunting perfumed nose, power on the palate and none of the shrill acidity that so many 1996 Burgundies have.

Mongeard Mugneret Richebourg 1993 and 2001
If you wanted a little extra acid, I would look no further than the 1993 Richebourg. More of a characteristic than a flaw, this was all about fruit and spice, an abundance of clove and licorice. Bright medium to long finish. The 2001 was still a little backward, but again it had a good deal of fruit, a spice overlay and a very long finish. One of the few wines along with the 1998 Lafleur that, I felt, was not fully mature.

Dessert wines
There were three; the middle one so volatile that I did not want to taste it (and never noted what it was) for fear of ruining my palate for the Yquem.

Royal Tokaji Essencia 1999 and Yquem 1988
The Tokaji is something totally different and if you have not tried, I would urge you do so. You don’t need much, historically the serving vessel was a spoon. The texture is thick and unctuous, pure raisins highlighted by sparks of apricot. It’s delicious, but my small glass was more than enough.
The Yquem was fresher and quite lovely. My palate was a little beaten up by the Essencia so no notes except a thumbs up

link_counts.title: High end bottles that are worth it

posts.name: Jim Cowan
posts.cooked: Damn!

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
There was a lot of discussion as to whether to drink the Bordeaux before the Burgundies, and that is what we ended up doing. Served with a risotto of confit duck and oyster mushroom, again a fine match.

Great tasting. Thanks for the wonderful notes.

I agree with your decision to drink Bordeaux before Burgundy. I have had experiences (not all the time) where Bordeauxs for some reason taste flat after Burgundies and in my experience it has never hurt a Burgundy to be drunk after Bordeaux. The one issue sometimes is that one wants the Bordeauxs with a later course (say beef) than the Burgs, but if it can be worked out with the menu, what you did is my preference as well.

posts.name: Brent Clayton
posts.cooked: Wow, wish I had been on your shoulder for the 72 La Tache. Well done Mark.

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked:
Someone has to say it — excesibe ballas

Great report.

posts.name: alan weinberg
posts.cooked: great writing. Loved “in a minor key,” and other comments. I have been eyeing an 01 M-M Richebourg, will now let it rest.

posts.name: Nick Gangas
posts.cooked: loved those old Ponelles. Have a couple 78 BM left.

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
Mark, thank you for writing up the notes! We’re generally in agreement, though I would have been less kind to the 72 Drouhin and much kinder to the 86 Echezeaux, which I thought was a fabulous bottle that was overshadowed by the spectacular 72. On the other hand, the difference between the two Dujacs was quite stark for me, and reminds me of the pre/post 99 Chave dichotomy. That 96 Dujac Clos St. Denis may be the best 1996 red Burgundy I’ve ever had, a vintage where the acid has badly outlasted the fruit.

I suspect you’re more sensitive to VA than I am, as you’re being a bit unkind to the third dessert wine - Stuart’s 1939(!!!) Passito, which was very much alive and which I thought was a very old German TBA blind.

posts.name: Jeff Leve
posts.cooked: I am 100% with you on the 98 Lafleur. it has morphed into a compelling wine that is now drinking just great.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
Mark, thank you for writing up the notes! We’re generally in agreement, though I would have been less kind to the 72 Drouhin and much kinder to the 86 Echezeaux, which I thought was a fabulous bottle that was overshadowed by the spectacular 72. On the other hand, the difference between the two Dujacs was quite stark for me, and reminds me of the pre/post 99 Chave dichotomy. That 96 Dujac Clos St. Denis may be the best 1996 red Burgundy I’ve ever had, a vintage where the acid has badly outlasted the fruit.

I suspect you’re more sensitive to VA than I am, as you’re being a bit unkind to the third dessert wine - Stuart’s 1939(!!!) Passito, which was very much alive and which I thought was a very old German TBA blind.

Very sensitive to VA; don’t drink Madeira and the black vinegar served with Chinese soup dumplings make me want to gag. I can’t say I have ever had a German wine with anything close to the level of volatility of that 1939.

Agree on the Dujac; the 1996 is pretty stunning.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
I am 100% with you on the 98 Lafleur. it has morphed into a compelling wine that is now drinking just great.

It drinks beautifully now, but I think it will get better with a little more time in the cellar.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I am 100% with you on the 98 Lafleur. it has morphed into a compelling wine that is now drinking just great.

It drinks beautifully now, but I think it will get better with a little more time in the cellar.

The 1998 is great, but the Lafleur you need to open right now is the 1999!

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
I am 100% with you on the 98 Lafleur. it has morphed into a compelling wine that is now drinking just great.

It drinks beautifully now, but I think it will get better with a little more time in the cellar.

The 1998 is great, but the Lafleur you need to open right now is the 1999!

I thought what made the 98 much better was the potential, not the current state. The 88 was more ready but was more coarse - the 98 had such beautiful pure fruit.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
I am 100% with you on the 98 Lafleur. it has morphed into a compelling wine that is now drinking just great.

Wow. What is happening. A couple of weeks ago, you and Alfert agreed and now you and Mark do.

posts.name: Jon Lawrence
posts.cooked:
For many years, the first Friday of the year was set aside for our Over the Top lunch. it was a chance to pull out those bottles that were too precious or expensive to drink on a weekday, and the pain would be less if shared with friends.
We were younger then, with far greater capacity, or hollower legs, so we all brought two bottles, one the special one, the other something interesting which allowed the geek in us full rein. With 30 plus bottles, lunches tended to go on for five hours or so, either that or until they kicked us out. They petered out; a logistic and time sink hole.

I miss those OTT lunches!! Every one I went to was outstanding. I know it was a lot of work for you and Bob to organize. Thanks Mark.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
It drinks beautifully now, but I think it will get better with a little more time in the cellar.

The 1998 is great, but the Lafleur you need to open right now is the 1999!

I thought what made the 98 much better was the potential, not the current state. The 88 was more ready but was more coarse - the 98 had such beautiful pure fruit.

Chez Lafleur, they prefer the 1999 to the 1998. I don’t think they are wrong.

I have not had the 99 but am very willing to be convinced!

Chez Lafleur, they prefer the 1999 to the 1998. I don’t think they are wrong.

While I like the 99, I love the 98. I see a big difference in quality between the two vintages in all of Bordeaux, as well as at Lafleur. Recent tasting notes of both vintages - Learn about Chateau Lafleur Pomerol Wine, Complete Guide

Damn!

x2. A lifetime of pinnacle wines

posts.name: Sarah Smith
posts.cooked:
I have had only a few birthday celebrations in my life that were truly special. When I was eight years old I had the best party possible for an eight year old- we went to Splashdown waterslide; I had a piñata; and my friends stayed for a sleepover. When I turned 40 I flew to Chicago to dine at Alinea and stayed at the Waldorf. And for my 50th, someone I had never met in person hosted the OTT lunch based on a wine he had procured for me for my 50th birthday: the 1972 La Tache mentioned in his original post.

For all of his kindness I can not post my tasting notes in full as he requested. I had a difficult time wrapping my head around all of these new and beautiful wines- two, three at a time, each one brilliant in its own right. To say that the afternoon was merely an “education” would be to deny how incredibly sensual the experience of tasting all of these wines on a beautiful summer afternoon with exceptional company was for this “neophyte.”

I promised that I would make a post, so please take my notes with a grain of salt. Some wines that stood out:

2006 Krug: I have never had a champagne that comes even close. To me it was just soft and beautiful, and made me catch my breath.

2009 Coche: I couldn’t believe this wine was real, and was thrilled that the owner was kind enough to bring a second bottle. This is the first white wine that truly took me to another place. As an aside, my white wine experience is even MORE limited than my red wine experience, and my first taste of (red) Burgundy was shipped to me by Mark not much more than a year ago. I think some of the others at my table commented that they could actually tell by watching me close my eyes and from my expression that I was enthralled. Not very good at this, but white peach, something mineral-y, and thyme stood out. (Isn’t Peaches and Herb a band? No I did not drink this and think “Shake Your Groove Thing.” More like “Reunited.” )

1989 Lafite: I could smell this all day. The nose was beautiful and floral. Of all of the wines, this one is the one I’d make into a scratch and sniff if I could. Though lovely, I would not say that it was my favorite to taste as much as to smell. There’s your note: “top candidate for a child’s sticker.” I joke…except that it would be amazing to be able to smell this wine on demand!

1972 La Tache: Finally, my wine! Well, I did a stellar job on my first attempt to pour for a large group: I managed to pour the bottle perfectly, only running out when I got back to my own glass. Oops! My new friends on either side were kind enough to each give me some of theirs, so crisis averted! I would say that this was a perfect wine, from an intellectual standpoint. I had a hard time believing that it was nearly 50 years old, but it was delicious. Still, not quite my favorite.

“The” Dujac was my favorite. Which one? I wish I knew. I know, terrible, and I should be shunned. I can’t remember which Dujac was in which of two glasses, sadly, but one definitely stood out. It was the most elegant wine I have ever tasted, period.

Thank you Mark, for hosting this wonderful afternoon, for giving someone you had never met a celebration to remember, and for introducing me to a quality and beauty in wine that elevates it to art.

links.title: High end bottles that are worth it
